county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Some early Umpqua County records are fragmentary.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Umpqua County in 1852 (Umpqua County was abolished, and Douglas absorbed it). Named for Stephen A. Douglas, U.S. Senator from Illinois. Roseburg has been the county seat since 1854. Major timber-producing county in the Umpqua River valley. The Southern Oregon Wagon Road and Oregon & California Railroad brought settlers. The 1959 Roseburg Blast (ammunition truck explosion) damaged downtown but not the courthouse.
county_metadata.research_tips: Douglas County absorbed Umpqua County in 1862, so researchers must check both county names for early records. Umpqua County records (1851–1862) are at the Oregon State Archives and partly at the Douglas County Clerk's office. The county clerk in Roseburg has deed and marriage records from 1852. The Douglas County Museum of History and Natural History in Roseburg holds extensive timber industry records, family files, and photograph collections — one of Oregon's best county museums. Donation Land Claims for Douglas County settlers are at BLM GLO. The Cow Creek Band of Umpqua Tribe of Indians is headquartered in Roseburg — tribal records and BIA files at NARA Seattle document this community. FamilySearch has microfilmed Douglas County records. The 1959 Roseburg Blast destroyed some downtown business records but courthouse records survived.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; limited online access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes Umpqua Valley timber land, Donation Land Claims, and homestead entries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — includes absorbed Umpqua County records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Donation Land Claim files for Umpqua Valley settlers with detailed biographical data.

source_profiles.source_name: Cow Creek Band of Umpqua Tribe — BIA Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for the Cow Creek Band including treaty records, census rolls, and termination files. Tribe terminated 1956, restored 1982.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA Seattle holds western Oregon BIA agency records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Includes Umpqua County-era marriages.
Federal census 1860–1950 (1890 destroyed). Includes former Umpqua County enumeration areas.

source_profiles.source_name: Roseburg News-Review and Douglas County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Roseburg and Umpqua Valley newspapers covering timber industry, railroad, and community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records well preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Wasco County in 1885. Named for Colonel Cornelius Gilliam, commander of Oregon Provisional Government forces in the Cayuse War (1848). Condon has been the county seat since formation. One of Oregon's least populated counties. Wheat farming and cattle ranching dominate. The John Day River runs through the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Gilliam County is one of Oregon's smallest counties by population, which means records are relatively manageable but also sparse. The county clerk in Condon has deed and marriage records from 1885. For pre-1885 records of the Condon area, check Wasco County (parent). Homestead patent records at BLM GLO document the dryland wheat farmers who settled the county. The Gilliam County Historical Society has family files and photographs documenting ranching and wheat farming families. FamilySearch has limited microfilm for Gilliam County. The Oregon Trunk Railway (completed 1911) through the Deschutes River canyon brought economic changes — railroad employment records may be at Oregon Historical Society. Stock brand records at the state level help trace ranching families. The county's remote location means fewer church records than in western Oregon.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; in-office access only

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Wheat farming homesteads and ranching land transactions.
Federal land patents for dryland wheat farming homesteads in the Columbia Plateau.
Marriage records from county formation.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — small volume but continuous from 1885.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1890–1950 (1890 destroyed). Small population — entire county easily searchable.

source_profiles.source_name: Condon Globe-Times and Gilliam County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Condon newspapers covering wheat farming, ranching, and rural community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Limited digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Some early mining camp records are scattered.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Wasco and Umatilla counties in 1864 during the eastern Oregon gold rush. Named for Ulysses S. Grant. Canyon City, a gold mining town, has been the county seat since formation. The John Day gold rush (1862) brought thousands of miners including a significant Chinese population. The Kam Wah Chung building in John Day was a Chinese medicine shop and social center (1866–1948).
county_metadata.research_tips: Grant County's research centers on the John Day gold rush of 1862 and subsequent ranching economy. Canyon City and John Day were major mining centers with significant Chinese populations — the Kam Wah Chung State Heritage Site in John Day preserves one of the most important Chinese immigrant sites in the American West, and its records document Chinese miners, merchants, and herbalist patients from across eastern Oregon. Chinese Exclusion Act case files at NARA Seattle cover Grant County Chinese residents. The county clerk in Canyon City has deed, mining claim, and marriage records from 1864. The Grant County Historical Museum in Canyon City has gold mining artifacts and family files. FamilySearch has microfilmed Grant County records. BLM GLO records document homestead entries for the ranching era that followed the gold rush. The Malheur National Forest records at NARA include timber sale files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed and mining claim records from formation; in-office access

source_profiles.source_name: Grant County Clerk — Land and Mining Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records and mining claims from gold rush era through ranching period.

source_profiles.source_name: Kam Wah Chung and Chinese Community Records
source_profiles.repository: Kam Wah Chung State Heritage Site / Oregon State Parks
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records from the Kam Wah Chung building including herbalist patient records, business correspondence, and Chinese community documentation from eastern Oregon gold mining towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Exceptional — the building was sealed in 1948 and contents preserved intact.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Gold rush era records include diverse mining community names.
Federal census 1870–1950 (1890 destroyed). 1870 and 1880 censuses show large Chinese mining population.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records are well preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Grant County in 1889. Named for General William S. Harney. Burns has been the county seat since formation. Oregon's largest county by area (over 10,000 square miles). High desert ranching country with the Malheur National Wildlife Refuge and Steens Mountain. The Burns Paiute Tribe reservation is located here. Peter French's P Ranch was one of the largest cattle operations in the West.
county_metadata.research_tips: Harney County is Oregon's largest county by area but has always had a small population, making records manageable but sparse. The county clerk in Burns has deed and marriage records from 1889. For pre-1889 records, check Grant County (parent). The Harney County Historical Society in Burns has ranching family files, photographs, and oral histories. Peter French's P Ranch records (1872–1897) are significant for early ranching history — French was murdered in 1897 and the subsequent legal records are at the state archives. The Burns Paiute Tribe has its own records, and BIA records at NARA Seattle cover the Burns Paiute Reservation. Malheur National Wildlife Refuge records document early homesteaders whose land became the refuge. BLM GLO homestead patents cover the desert homesteading era. FamilySearch has limited microfilm for Harney County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; in-office access only

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes massive ranching land transactions and desert homestead entries.
BIA records for the Burns Paiute community including census rolls, allotment records, and agency correspondence.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA Seattle holds BIA records for Oregon agencies.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Small volume reflects sparse population.
Federal land patents for ranching homesteads and desert land entries in Oregon's largest county.
Federal census 1890–1950 (1890 destroyed). Very small population throughout.

source_profiles.source_name: Burns Times-Herald and Harney County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Burns area newspapers covering ranching, land disputes, and high desert community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Limited digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records begin at county formation; earlier records in Wasco County.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Wasco County in 1908. Named for the Hood River, a tributary of the Columbia. The town of Hood River has been the county seat since formation. Located in the Columbia River Gorge, the county became famous for fruit orcharding, particularly apples and pears. Significant Japanese farming community (Issei settlers from 1900s) — families were forcibly removed during WWII internment. WWII internment and subsequent property loss documented in federal records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hood River County is genealogically notable for its Japanese farming community, one of the most significant in Oregon. Japanese Issei families settled here from the early 1900s, purchasing orchards and developing the fruit industry. During WWII, Japanese Americans were forcibly removed to internment camps, and the Hood River American Legion infamously struck Japanese American names from the war memorial — this history is well-documented. Japanese internment records are at NARA Washington (War Relocation Authority files), and the Oregon Nikkei Endowment in Portland has compiled community histories. The county clerk has deed and marriage records from 1908. For pre-1908 records, check Wasco County. The Hood River County Historical Museum has fruit industry records and immigrant community documentation. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. BLM GLO records cover timber homesteads in the upper Hood River valley.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; limited online index

source_profiles.source_name: Hood River County Clerk — Land Records
source_profiles.repository: Hood River County Clerk, Hood River
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes orchard land transactions and Japanese farmer land purchases and forced sales during WWII.

source_profiles.source_name: Japanese American Farming Community and WWII Internment Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: War Relocation Authority records for Hood River Japanese Americans, property records documenting forced sales, and community histories of Issei and Nisei farming families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds WRA files. Oregon Nikkei Endowment has compiled community histories and oral histories.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Pre-1908 marriages filed in Wasco County.
Federal census from 1910 (first after county formation). Pre-1908 area enumerated under Wasco County. Shows Japanese farming families.

source_profiles.source_name: Hood River Glacier and Hood River News

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Jacksonville (original county seat) records transferred to Medford in 1927.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Lane County in 1852 during the southern Oregon gold rush. Named for President Andrew Jackson. Jacksonville was the original county seat (1852–1927); seat moved to Medford when the railroad bypassed Jacksonville. Jacksonville is now a National Historic Landmark. The Rogue River Indian Wars (1853, 1855–1856) resulted in forced removal of Native peoples to the Siletz and Grand Ronde reservations. Southern Oregon's commercial center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jackson County research splits between the Jacksonville gold rush era (1852–1927) and the Medford agricultural/commercial era. When the county seat moved to Medford in 1927, records were transferred — but researchers should verify which office holds specific older records. The Jacksonville Cemetery is one of the oldest in southern Oregon with burials from the 1850s gold rush. The Southern Oregon Historical Society in Medford/Jacksonville has extensive collections including gold mining records, family files, photographs, and Rogue Valley history. The Jackson County Clerk has deed records from 1852 including gold mining claims. Rogue River Indian War records (1853, 1855–1856) at NARA document both soldiers and the Native peoples displaced to reservations. Donation Land Claims for Rogue Valley settlers are at BLM GLO. FamilySearch has microfilmed Jackson County records. The Table Rock Reservation records predate the forced removal to Siletz.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; online index available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records and mining claims from Jacksonville gold rush era. Records transferred from Jacksonville to Medford in 1927.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete — records survived county seat transfer.

source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds the federal military records. Oregon State Archives has volunteer militia records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Gold rush era records include diverse mining camp populations.

source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — Jacksonville Cemetery is well-documented. Historical society has transcribed many Rogue Valley cemeteries.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records begin at county formation; earlier records in Crook County.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Crook County in 1914. Named for Mount Jefferson. Madras has been the county seat since formation. The Confederated Tribes of Warm Springs Reservation occupies the western portion of the county — one of Oregon's largest reservations. Central Oregon ranching and farming county. The Crooked River and Deschutes River canyons are prominent geographic features.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jefferson County research has two distinct tracks: the non-reservation ranching and farming communities around Madras, and the Confederated Tribes of Warm Springs Reservation (Warm Springs, Wasco, and Paiute peoples). The county clerk in Madras has deed and marriage records from 1914. For pre-1914 records, check Crook County (parent) and before 1882, Wasco County. The Warm Springs tribal records are maintained by the tribe, and BIA records at NARA Seattle document the reservation community — treaty records (1855), allotment records, census rolls, and agency correspondence are key sources. The Museum at Warm Springs has cultural and genealogical resources. Homestead patent records at BLM GLO cover non-reservation land entries. FamilySearch has limited microfilm for Jefferson County. The North Unit Irrigation District records document water rights for farming families arriving after 1940.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; limited online access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes ranch land and irrigated farmland transactions.

source_profiles.source_name: Confederated Tribes of Warm Springs — BIA and Treaty Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA Seattle / Museum at Warm Springs
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for the Warm Springs, Wasco, and Paiute peoples. Treaty records (1855), census rolls, allotment records, and Warm Springs Agency correspondence.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA Seattle holds BIA records. Museum at Warm Springs has cultural materials.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from county formation. Pre-1914 marriages filed in Crook County.
Federal census from 1920 (first after county formation). Pre-1914 area enumerated under Crook County. Warm Springs Reservation has separate BIA census rolls.

source_profiles.source_name: Madras Pioneer and Jefferson County Newspapers

source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records are well preserved from county formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Jackson County in 1856. Named for Josephine Rollins, reportedly the first white woman in the area (or for Josephine Creek). Grants Pass has been the county seat since 1886 (earlier seat at Kerbyville/Kerby). Gold mining in the Illinois Valley and along Josephine Creek was the initial industry. The Rogue River and Illinois River valleys define the geography.
county_metadata.research_tips: Josephine County's gold mining era (1850s–1880s) is central to early research, and the county seat move from Kerby to Grants Pass in 1886 means older records may need verification of location. The county clerk in Grants Pass has deed and mining claim records from 1856. Kerbyville (now Kerby) was the original county seat and the Kerbyville Museum has gold mining artifacts and local history. Chinese miners were present in the Illinois Valley gold fields — check NARA Seattle for Chinese Exclusion Act case files. Donation Land Claims for Josephine County are at BLM GLO. The Josephine County Historical Society has family files and photographs. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Grants Pass became important when the Oregon & California Railroad arrived in the 1880s. The Illinois Valley area (Cave Junction) was a significant mining and later timber region with its own distinct community records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; limited online index

source_profiles.source_name: Josephine County Clerk — Land and Mining Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records and mining claims from county formation including Illinois Valley gold mining district.
Donation Land Claim files for Rogue River and Illinois Valley settlers.
Marriage records from county formation.
Federal census 1860–1950 (1890 destroyed). Shows gold mining communities and later timber/agricultural population.

source_profiles.source_name: Grants Pass Courier and Josephine County Newspapers
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grants Pass and Illinois Valley newspapers covering mining, timber, and community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial digitization; microfilm at Oregon State Library.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Records well preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed from Lake County in 1882. Named for the Klamath people. Klamath Falls (originally Linkville) has been the county seat since formation. The Klamath Tribes (Klamath, Modoc, Yahooskin Band of Snake Indians) were among the first tribes terminated under the federal termination policy (1954) — restored in 1986. The Klamath Basin irrigation project transformed the area. Fort Klamath was a significant military post (1863–1890).
county_metadata.research_tips: Klamath County research involves two major genealogical threads: the Klamath Tribes (terminated 1954, restored 1986) and the Euro-American ranching and irrigation farming communities. The Klamath Tribes were among the wealthiest tribes in Oregon due to timber resources before termination, and their records at NARA Seattle are extensive — including allotment records, timber payment rolls, and termination-era documentation that lists every tribal member. The county clerk in Klamath Falls has deed records from 1882. Fort Klamath military records (1863–1890) at NARA document both soldiers and the Modoc War (1872–1873) including Captain Jack's capture and execution. The Klamath County Museum has local history collections. The Klamath Basin irrigation project records (post-1905) at Bureau of Reclamation/NARA document homesteaders. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check Lake County (parent) for pre-1882 records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed records from formation; online index available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes Klamath Basin irrigation project homesteads and tribal allotment records.

source_profiles.source_name: Klamath Tribes — BIA, Allotment, and Termination Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Comprehensive BIA records for the Klamath, Modoc, and Yahooskin peoples. Includes treaty records (1864), allotment files, timber payment rolls, and termination rolls (1954) listing every tribal member. Tribe restored in 1986.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent — NARA Seattle holds extensive records. Termination-era rolls are particularly detailed.

source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — NARA holds the federal military records.
